description:
You are helping a junior penetration-tester prepare a repeatable local working
    environment.  Carry out the following steps from a normal user account
    ( /home/user ) without root privileges.

    1.  Create a directory that will hold all scan material.
        • Full path: /home/user/pentest-workspace

    2.  Inside that directory, create the target list file exactly as shown
        below.  The file must be plain text with a single line-feed
        (<LF>, “\n”) at the end of each line.

        File: /home/user/pentest-workspace/targets.txt
        ─────────────────────────────────────
        192.168.0.10
        192.168.0.20
        192.168.0.30
        ─────────────────────────────────────

    3.  Define a convenience alias so the tester can immediately launch a
        standard service-detection scan against all targets and log the output.
        Add the alias to the user’s alias file, creating the file if it does
        not yet exist.

        File to edit: /home/user/.bash_aliases  
        Exact line that must be present (including all spaces and quotes):

        alias vulnscan='nmap -sV -iL /home/user/pentest-workspace/targets.txt -oN /home/user/pentest-workspace/latest_scan.log'

    4.  Expose the workspace location to future shells by exporting an
        environment variable.  Append the following single line to the end of
        /home/user/.bashrc (do not modify other lines):

        export PENTEST_DIR="/home/user/pentest-workspace"

    5.  Self-check.  After all configuration steps are finished, create a
        three-line verification log at  
        /home/user/pentest_config_check.log  
        with the exact structure below.  Each key must be followed by either
        “yes” or “no” (lower-case), indicating whether the requirement was met
        by your commands:

            DIR_EXISTS: <yes|no>
            ALIAS_SET: <yes|no>
            VAR_SET:   <yes|no>

        • DIR_EXISTS  → “yes” if /home/user/pentest-workspace exists.  
        • ALIAS_SET   → “yes” if /home/user/.bash_aliases contains the alias
                        line precisely as given in step 3.  
        • VAR_SET     → “yes” if /home/user/.bashrc contains exactly the export
                        line shown in step 4.

    The automated tester will fail the exercise unless:

        a.  /home/user/pentest-workspace exists and is a directory.  
        b.  /home/user/pentest-workspace/targets.txt matches the three IP
            addresses above, one per line, nothing else.  
        c.  /home/user/.bash_aliases contains the alias line exactly, with no
            extra or missing characters.  
        d.  /home/user/.bashrc contains the export line exactly, with no extra
            or missing characters.  
        e.  /home/user/pentest_config_check.log is present and its three lines
            match the required keys and the word “yes” for each item.

    Do not output anything other than normal shell interaction while you work.
    Remember that new shells are NOT started automatically during the test, so
    focus on modifying the dotfiles themselves; sourcing them is optional.
truth:
<!-- Ground-truth used by the grader to validate the agent’s work.  
         DO NOT reveal in the public task description. -->

    1.  Directory that must exist:
        /home/user/pentest-workspace

    2.  File /home/user/pentest-workspace/targets.txt
        Exact 3 lines (each terminated by \n):
            192.168.0.10\n
            192.168.0.20\n
            192.168.0.30\n

    3.  File /home/user/.bash_aliases must contain the exact line:
        alias vulnscan='nmap -sV -iL /home/user/pentest-workspace/targets.txt -oN /home/user/pentest-workspace/latest_scan.log'

    4.  File /home/user/.bashrc must contain the exact line:
        export PENTEST_DIR="/home/user/pentest-workspace"

    5.  File /home/user/pentest_config_check.log
        Must contain exactly these 3 lines (each terminated by \n):
            DIR_EXISTS: yes\n
            ALIAS_SET: yes\n
            VAR_SET:   yes\n
